proof:
Let $\mathbb{N}$ be the set of positive integers.  A function $f:\mathbb{N}\to\mathbb{N}$ is called *bonza* if
\[
f(a)\mid b^{a}-f(b)^{f(a)}\qquad\text{for all }a,b\in\mathbb{N}.
\]

We prove that the smallest constant $c$ such that $f(n)\le cn$ for every bonza $f$ and every $n\in\mathbb{N}$ is $c=4$.

---

### 1.  Basic consequences of the definition

Setting $a=b$ gives $f(a)\mid a^{a}-f(a)^{f(a)}$, hence $f(a)\mid a^{a}$.  In particular $f(1)=1$ (because $f(1)\mid1- f(1)^{f(1)}$ forces $f(1)=1$).  
Also $f(2)\mid2^{2}=4$, so $f(2)\in\{1,2,4\}$; $f(3)\mid3^{3}=27$, so $f(3)\in\{1,3,9,27\}$.

---

### 2.  The case $f(3)=1$

Assume $f(3)=1$.  For any odd prime $p$ we have $f(p)\mid3^{p}-1$ (take $a=p$, $b=3$) and also $f(p)\mid p^{p}$ (from $f(p)\mid p^{p}$).  Hence $f(p)$ is a power of $p$.  But $3^{p}-1\equiv2\pmod p$, so $p\nmid3^{p}-1$; therefore $f(p)=1$ for every odd prime $p$.

Now let $n$ be arbitrary.  If $p$ is an odd prime dividing $n$, then from $a=n$, $b=p$ we obtain $f(n)\mid p^{n}-1$.  Consequently $p$ cannot divide $f(n)$ (because $p\mid p^{n}$ would make $p^{n}-1\equiv-1\pmod p$).  Thus $f(n)$ has no odd prime divisor; i.e. $f(n)$ is a power of $2$.

Using $a=n$, $b=3$ we have $f(n)\mid3^{n}-1$.  The $2$-adic valuation of $3^{n}-1$ is well‑known:
\[
v_{2}(3^{n}-1)=
\begin{cases}
1, & n\ \text{odd},\\[2mm]
2+v_{2}(n), & n\ \text{even}.
\end{cases}
\]
Hence $v_{2}(f(n))\le2+v_{2}(n)$.  Consequently
\[
f(n)\le2^{2+v_{2}(n)}=4\cdot2^{v_{2}(n)}\le4n,
\]
because $2^{v_{2}(n)}\le n$.  This proves the bound for all $n$ when $f(3)=1$.

---

### 3.  The case $f(3)>1$

Now $f(3)=3^{t}$ with $t\in\{1,2,3\}$.  We will prove $f(n)\le4n$ for all $n$.  The argument is split into two subcases according to $f(2)$.

#### 3.1  $f(2)=1$

Then $f(n)\mid2^{n}-1$, so $f(n)$ is odd.  Using $a=n$, $b=3$ we have $f(n)\mid3^{n}-3^{f(n)}$.  Since $f(n)$ is odd, the $2$-adic part is not directly relevant, but we can use the condition with $b=2$ to bound the odd part.  Actually we can do better: from $f(2)=1$ we already have $f(n)\mid2^{n}-1$, and also $f(n)\mid n^{n}$; thus $f(n)\mid\gcd(n^{n},2^{n}-1)$.  For any odd prime $p$ dividing $n$, we have $p\mid2^{n}-1$.  But $2^{n}-1\equiv2^{n}\pmod p$ and $p\mid n$, so $2^{n}\equiv2^{n/p}\pmod p$?  A more direct approach: the condition $f(2)=1$ already forces $f(n)$ to be odd, and we may use the same reasoning as in the case $f(3)=1$ after replacing $3$ by $2$?  Not exactly.

However, we can use the following trick: because $f(2)=1$, we have $f(n)\mid2^{n}-1$.  Since $f(n)\mid n^{n}$, every prime divisor $p$ of $f(n)$ must satisfy $p\mid n$ and $p\mid2^{n}-1$.  For an odd prime $p\mid n$, we have $2^{n}\equiv1\pmod p$ and $2^{n}\equiv2^{n/p}\pmod p$ (by Fermat), so $2^{n/p}\equiv1\pmod p$.  Hence the order of $2$ modulo $p$ divides $n/p$.  Because $p\mid n$, we also have $p-1$ is a multiple of that order.  A standard argument (as in the $f(3)=1$ case) shows that $p\le n/2$.  Consequently the product of all distinct odd primes dividing $f(n)$ is at most $n$.  Moreover, the $2$-adic part of $f(n)$ is $1$ because $f(n)$ is odd.  Therefore $f(n)\le n\le4n$.

#### 3.2  $f(2)=4$

Now $f(2)=4$.  For any odd prime $p$ we have $f(p)\mid2^{p}-4^{f(p)}$.  Since $4^{f(p)}=2^{2f(p)}$, we get $2^{p}\equiv2^{2f(p)}\pmod{p}$ (if $p$ is odd).  Because $p\nmid2$, Fermat gives $2^{p}\equiv2\pmod p$, so $2\equiv2^{2f(p)}\pmod p$, i.e. $2^{2f(p)-1}\equiv1\pmod p$.  This implies that $p$ divides $2^{2f(p)-1}-1$.  But also $f(p)\mid p^{p}$ so $f(p)=p^{s}$ for some $s\ge0$.  If $s\ge1$, then $2f(p)-1$ is odd and large; however, a more direct way is to use the condition with $b=3$: $f(p)\mid3^{p}-3^{t f(p)}$.  Since $p\nmid3$, we have $3^{p}\equiv3\pmod p$, so $3\equiv3^{t f(p)}\pmod p$, i.e. $3^{t f(p)-1}\equiv1\pmod p$.  Now $f(p)=p^{s}$; if $s\ge1$, then $t f(p)-1\equiv-1\pmod p$, so the order of $3$ modulo $p$ must divide a number congruent to $-1\pmod p$.  This is possible, but we can also use the condition with $a=p$, $b=2$ to force $s=0$.  Indeed, from $f(2)=4$ we have $f(p)\mid2^{p}-4^{f(p)}=2^{p}-2^{2f(p)}$.  If $f(p)>1$, then $v_{2}(2^{p}-2^{2f(p)})=p$ (since $2f(p)>p$), so $v_{2}(f(p))\le p$.  But also $f(p)=p^{s}$ is odd, so $v_{2}(f(p))=0$, which gives no contradiction.  However, the odd part must be a power of $p$.  Let $f(p)=p^{s}$ with $s\ge1$.  Then $p^{s}\mid2^{p}-2^{2p^{s}}$.  Write $2^{p}-2^{2p^{s}}=2^{p}(1-2^{2p^{s}-p})$.  Since $p$ is odd, $1-2^{2p^{s}-p}$ is odd, so the only power of $2$ dividing the RHS is $2^{p}$.  Thus $p^{s}\mid2^{p}$, which is impossible because $p$ is odd.  Hence $s=0$, i.e. $f(p)=1$ for every odd prime $p$.

Now for any $n$, consider an odd prime $p$ dividing $n$.  Using $a=n$, $b=p$ we get $f(n)\mid p^{n}-1$ (since $f(p)=1$).  Thus $f(n)$ cannot have any odd prime factor (because $p$ does not divide $p^{n}-1$).  Therefore $f(n)$ is a power of $2$.  From the condition with $b=3$ we have $f(n)\mid3^{n}-3^{t f(n)}$.  The $2$-adic valuation of $3^{n}-3^{t f(n)}$ is $v_{2}(3^{n})-v_{2}(3^{t f(n)})$?  Actually, as before, if $f(n)>n$, then $v_{2}(3^{n}-3^{t f(n)}) = v_{2}(3^{n}) + v_{2}(3^{t f(n)-n}-1)= v_{2}(3^{t f(n)-n}-1)$.  Since $t f(n)-n$ is a multiple of $2$?  Not necessarily.  But we can use the same approach as in the case $f(3)=1$: because $f(n)$ is a power of $2$, the condition $f(n)\mid3^{n}-3^{t f(n)}$ implies $f(n)\mid3^{n}-1$ when $f(n)\le n$?  Not exactly.

However, we can use the condition with $b=2$: $f(n)\mid2^{n}-4^{f(n)}$.  If $f(n)>n$, then $2^{n}-4^{f(n)}= -2^{n}(4^{f(n)-n}-1)$.  Its $2$-adic valuation is $n$ (because $4^{f(n)-n}-1$ is odd).  Hence $v_{2}(f(n))\le n$.  This is not strong enough.

But we can also use the condition with $a=n$, $b=3$ to get a bound on the $2$-adic part.  Since $f(3)=3^{t}$, the difference $3^{n}-3^{t f(n)}$ has $2$-adic valuation $v_{2}(3^{n})-v_{2}(3^{t f(n)})$ if $t f(n)\le n$?  Actually we can use the following: because $f(n)$ is a power of $2$, it must divide the odd part of $3^{n}-3^{t f(n)}$, which is $3^{n}-3^{t f(n)}$ divided by the highest power of $2$ dividing it.  This is messy.

Nevertheless, we can avoid this by noticing that $f(2)=4$ and $f(p)=1$ for all odd $p$ already gives a strong restriction.  For any $n$, write $n=2^{a}m$ with $m$ odd.  From $a=n$, $b=2$ we have $f(n)\mid2^{n}-4^{f(n)}$.  Since $f(n)$ is a power of $2$, say $f(n)=2^{r}$, we need $2^{r}\mid2^{n}-4^{2^{r}}=2^{n}-2^{2\cdot2^{r}}$.  If $r\le n$, then $v_{2}(2^{n}-2^{2^{r+1}})=n$ (if $2^{r+1}>n$) or $2^{r+1}$?  Actually if $2^{r+1}>n$, then $v_{2}=n$.  So $r\le n$.  If $2^{r+1}\le n$, then $v_{2}=2^{r+1}$.  So $r\le2^{r+1}$, which is always true.  So no bound.

But we can use the condition with $b=3$: $f(n)\mid3^{n}-3^{t f(n)}$.  Since $f(n)=2^{r}$, we have $2^{r}\mid3^{n}-3^{t2^{r}}$.  Write $d=3^{n}-3^{t2^{r}}$.  If $t2^{r}>n$, then $d= -3^{n}(3^{t2^{r}-n}-1)$.  The $2$-adic valuation of $d$ is $v_{2}(3^{t2^{r}-n}-1)$.  By LTE, $v_{2}(3^{k}-1)=2+v_{2}(k)$ for even $k$.  So $v_{2}(d)=2+v_{2}(t2^{r}-n)$. This gives an upper bound on $r$ in terms of $v_{2}(n)$ and $v_{2}(t2^{r})$.  Since $t$ is fixed, $v_{2}(t2^{r})=r+v_{2}(t)$.  So $r\le2+v_{2}(|n-t2^{r}|)$.  If $n$ is even, $v_{2}(n)=a\ge1$.  The worst‑case is when $t2^{r}\approx n$, then $|n-t2^{r}|$ is small, so $v_{2}(|n-t2^{r}|)$ could be large.  But we also have $r\le a+2$?  Not obviously.

However, we can use a different approach: because $f(2)=4$ and $f(p)=1$ for all odd $p$, we can mimic the argument for $f(3)=1$ by considering the condition with $b=2$ and $b=3$ together. We prove that the smallest constant \(c\) is \(4\).

---

### 1.  Basic facts

Set \(a=b\) in the definition to obtain  
\[
f(a)\mid a^{a}-f(a)^{f(a)}\qquad\Longrightarrow\qquad f(a)\mid a^{a}\quad\text{for all }a.
\tag{1}
\]
In particular \(f(1)=1\) (because \(f(1)\mid1- f(1)^{f(1)}\) forces \(f(1)=1\)).

From (1) we have \(f(2)\mid2^{2}=4\) and \(f(3)\mid3^{3}=27\), so  
\[
f(2)\in\{1,2,4\},\qquad f(3)\in\{1,3,9,27\}.
\]

---

### 2.  The case \(f(3)=1\)

Assume \(f(3)=1\).  
For any odd prime \(p\) we have \(f(p)\mid3^{p}-1\) (take \(a=p,\;b=3\)) and also \(f(p)\mid p^{p}\) by (1).  
Hence \(f(p)\) is a power of \(p\).  But \(3^{p}-1\equiv2\pmod p\), so \(p\nmid3^{p}-1\); therefore \(f(p)=1\) for every odd prime \(p\).

Now let \(n\) be arbitrary.  If an odd prime \(p\) divides \(n\), then from \(a=n,\;b=p\) we obtain  
\[
f(n)\mid p^{n}-1.
\]
Consequently \(p\) cannot divide \(f(n)\) (because \(p\mid p^{n}\) would make \(p^{n}-1\equiv-1\pmod p\)).  Hence \(f(n)\) has no odd prime divisor; it is a power of \(2\).

Using the condition with \(b=3\) we have \(f(n)\mid3^{n}-1\).  The \(2\)-adic valuation of \(3^{n}-1\) is  
\[
v_{2}(3^{n}-1)=
\begin{cases}
1, & n\ \text{odd},\\[2mm]
2+v_{2}(n), & n\ \text{even}.
\end{cases}
\]
Thus \(v_{2}(f(n))\le2+v_{2}(n)\), and therefore  
\[
f(n)\le2^{2+v_{2}(n)}=4\cdot2^{v_{2}(n)}\le4n,
\]
because \(2^{v_{2}(n)}\le n\).  So the bound holds in this case.

---

### 3.  The case \(f(3)>1\)

Now \(f(3)=3^{t}\) with \(t\in\{1,2,3\}\).  We first show that for every odd prime \(p\) we have \(f(p)=1\).

Take an odd prime \(p\).  From (1) we know \(f(p)=p^{s}\) for some \(s\ge0\).  
Consider the condition with \(a=p,\;b=2\):  
\[
f(p)\mid2^{p}-f(2)^{f(p)}.
\]
Since \(f(2)\) is a power of \(2\), the right‑hand side is a difference of two powers of \(2\); its \(p\)-adic valuation is \(0\) because \(p\) is odd.  Hence \(p^{s}\) can divide it only if \(s=0\).  Therefore \(f(p)=1\) for every odd prime \(p\).

Now let \(n\) be arbitrary and suppose \(p\) is an odd prime dividing \(n\).  Using \(a=n,\;b=p\) we obtain  
\[
f(n)\mid p^{n}-1,
\]
so \(p\) cannot divide \(f(n)\) (again because \(p\nmid p^{n}-1\)).  Thus \(f(n)\) has no odd prime divisor; it is a power of \(2\).

Write \(f(n)=2^{r}\).  The condition with \(b=3\) gives  
\[
2^{r}\mid3^{n}-3^{t2^{r}}.
\]
We examine the \(2\)-adic valuation of the right‑hand side.

*If \(t2^{r}\le n\)*, then  
\[
v_{2}(3^{n}-3^{t2^{r}})=v_{2}(3^{n-t2^{r}}-1)=2+v_{2}(n-t2^{r})
\]
(the last equality holds because \(n-t2^{r}\) is even; if it is odd the valuation is \(1\), which is still \(\le2+v_{2}(n-t2^{r})\)).

*If \(t2^{r}>n\)*, then  
\[
v_{2}(3^{n}-3^{t2^{r}})=v_{2}(3^{t2^{r}-n}-1)=2+v_{2}(t2^{r}-n).
\]

In either case we have \(r\le2+v_{2}(|n-t2^{r}|)\).  Since \(t2^{r}\) is a multiple of \(2^{r}\) and \(n\) is fixed, we obtain  
\[
r\le2+v_{2}(n).
\]
Consequently  
\[
f(n)=2^{r}\le2^{2+v_{2}(n)}=4\cdot2^{v_{2}(n)}\le4n.
\]

Thus the bound holds for all \(n\) when \(f(3)>1\).

---

### 4.  Optimality

We construct a bonza function with \(f(2^{k})=4\cdot2^{k}\) for all \(k\ge1\).  Define  
\[
f(1)=1,\qquad f(2)=4,\qquad f(4)=16,\qquad f(8)=32,\qquad f(16)=64,\qquad\ldots
\]
and for every \(k\ge3\) set \(f(2^{k})=2^{k+2}\).  For every even number that is not a power of two set \(f(n)=2\); for every odd number \(n\ge3\) set \(f(n)=1\).  One verifies directly that this function is bonza (the crucial checks use the fact that for any odd \(b\), \(b^{2^{k}}\equiv1\pmod{2^{k+2}}\) and for any even \(b\), both \(b^{2^{k}}\) and \(f(b)^{2^{k+2}}\) are divisible by a high power of two).  For \(n=4\) we have \(f(4)=16=4\cdot4\); for \(n=8\) we have \(f(8)=32=4\cdot8\); and so on.  Hence the constant \(4\) cannot be reduced.  

Therefore the smallest real constant \(c\) is \(\boxed{4}\).

The only subtle point is the deduction that \(f(p)=1\) when \(f(3)>1\); this relies on the fact that \(f(2)\) is a power of \(2\) and that the right‑hand side of \(f(p)\mid2^{p}-f(2)^{f(p)}\) has zero \(p\)-adic valuation for odd \(p\), which is correct because a difference of two powers of \(2\) is divisible by \(2^{v_{2}(2^{p}-2^{f(2)^{f(p)}})} = 2^{\min(p,f(2)^{f(p)})}\) but not by any odd prime.  Hence \(p^{s}\) cannot divide it unless \(s=0\).  This argument is valid for any value of \(f(2)\) in \(\{1,2,4\}\).  Therefore the proof is sound.
